does: This paper develops a framework for deciding when large language models should invoke external tools like web search. The authors propose three key decision factors—necessity (whether a tool call is needed), utility (whether it improves the response), and affordability (whether the cost justifies the benefit)—inspired by rational decision-making theory. They evaluate this framework on six open-source and one closed-source frontier model across two experimental settings, using both normative analysis that identifies optimal tool allocation patterns and descriptive analysis that reveals what models actually perceive about their own need for tools. The work identifies systematic gaps between when models think they need external information and when such calls are actually beneficial.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature around agentic AI systems that augment language models with tool access, extending prior research on tool use and agent design. It responds to an underexplored problem in tool-augmented LLM architectures: the decision of whether to invoke tools at all, rather than just how to use them once invoked. The framework draws on classical decision theory and cost-benefit analysis frameworks from economics and cognitive science. It also connects to recent work on prompt engineering and in-context learning for tool use, as well as research on model calibration and the gap between model confidence and actual performance, particularly regarding when models can leverage their internal knowledge versus when they genuinely need external information.
enables: This framework enables better design of agentic systems by providing principled methods to evaluate and optimize tool-calling decisions at deployment time. The normative and descriptive analysis perspectives could inform the development of training objectives that calibrate models to make better tool-use judgments. Future work could apply these insights to other tools beyond web search—databases, APIs, computation—and to multi-tool decision scenarios where models must choose among multiple available tools. The framework also opens questions about teaching models to more accurately self-assess their knowledge gaps and uncertainty, potentially through fine-tuning or retrieval-augmented generation approaches that improve the reliability of tool-calling decisions without human annotation.
